title: Search in 2D matrix
description: Search target value in sorted matrix

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize i=0 (first row [1,3,5,7]). Step 2: Initialize j=0, check matrix[0][0]=1 != 3. Step 3: j=1, check matrix[0][1]=3 == 3, match found. Step 4: Return True. Total comparisons: 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through every element in the 2D matrix to check if the target exists. Use nested loops to scan all rows and columns, making it straightforward but inefficient for large matrices.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Search in 2D Matrix
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of brute force search through every element in a sorted 2D matrix.

explanation.prose: The brute force approach to searching in a 2D matrix is the most straightforward method, though inefficient for large matrices. The intuition is simple: since we need to determine if a target value exists anywhere in the matrix, we can systematically examine every single element. This guarantees we won't miss the target if it exists, but requires checking all m×n elements in the worst case. The algorithm uses nested loops: an outer loop iterates through each row, and an inner loop iterates through each column within that row. For each element at position (i, j), we compare it with the target. If we find an exact match, we return True (or the position). If we complete all iterations without finding a match, we return False. This connects to the parent problem of matrix searching by providing a baseline solution that works regardless of matrix properties (though inefficient).

timeComplexity.note: where m is number of rows and n is number of columns, as we potentially visit every cell

spaceComplexity.note: since we only use a few variables for iteration and comparison

commonMistakes: Forgetting to handle empty matrices
Using wrong loop boundaries (off-by-one errors)
Returning too early without checking all elements
Confusing row and column indices
explanation.codePattern: def searchMatrix(matrix, target): for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[0])): if matrix[i][j] == target: return True return False

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0 (first row [1,3,5,7]). Step 2: Initialize j=0, check matrix[0][0]=1 != 3. Step 3: j=1, check matrix[0][1]=3 == 3, match found. Step 4: Return True. Total comparisons: 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Search Through a Single Row
ladders.desc: Search for a target value in a 1D array (row) using sequential checking.

explanation.prose: The key sub-routine in brute force matrix search is linear search through a single row. This is the fundamental operation that gets repeated for each row in the matrix. The intuition is straightforward: to find if a target exists in an array, we examine each element from start to end until we either find it or exhaust all elements. This works because we're systematically covering all possible positions. The algorithm: initialize an index variable (typically j) to 0. While j is less than the length of the row, compare the element at position j with the target. If they match, return True (or the index). If not, increment j and continue. If the loop completes without finding a match, return False. This feeds into the full matrix search by being the inner loop operation—for each row, we perform this linear search.

timeComplexity.note: where n is the row length, as we may check all elements

commonMistakes: Not handling empty rows
Using wrong comparison operators
Forgetting to increment the index, causing infinite loops
explanation.codePattern: def linearSearch(row, target): for j in range(len(row)): if row[j] == target: return True return False

examples.trace: Step 1: j=0, row[0]=1 ≠ 3. Step 2: j=1, row[1]=3 == 3, match found. Return True. Total comparisons: 2.
Step 1: j=0, 10≠13. Step 2: j=1, 11≠13. Step 3: j=2, 16≠13. Step 4: j=3, 20≠13. Step 5: All 4 elements checked, no match. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Iteration Over 2D Structure
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to systematically visit every element in a 2D grid.

explanation.prose: The core logic of brute force matrix search is using nested loops to iterate over all rows and columns. This is the central technique that enables checking every cell. The intuition: a 2D matrix can be thought of as rows of arrays. To visit all elements, we need two levels of iteration: an outer loop goes through each row (first dimension), and an inner loop goes through each column within that row (second dimension). This creates a systematic scan from top-left to bottom-right. The algorithm: initialize row index i to 0. While i is less than number of rows: initialize column index j to 0. While j is less than number of columns: access element at matrix[i][j], then increment j. After inner loop, increment i. This visits all m×n elements exactly once. This connects to the full problem by providing the iteration framework—once we can visit all elements, we can add the comparison logic.

timeComplexity.note: as we perform constant work per element

commonMistakes: Swapping row and column indices
Using wrong loop limits (e.g., using len(matrix) for columns)
Not resetting inner loop index for each row
explanation.codePattern: for i in range(len(matrix)): for j in range(len(matrix[0])): # access matrix[i][j]

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0 (row [1,2]). j=0: visit matrix[0][0]=1. j=1: visit matrix[0][1]=2. Step 2: i=1 (row [3,4]). j=0: visit matrix[1][0]=3. j=1: visit matrix[1][1]=4. Total visits: 4.
Step 1: i=0 (row [5,6,7]). j=0: visit 5. j=1: visit 6. j=2: visit 7. Total visits: 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Accessing Elements in a 2D Array
ladders.desc: Correctly retrieve elements from a 2D array using row and column indices.

explanation.prose: An intermediate concept needed for matrix search is accessing elements in a 2D array. This is the building block that allows us to examine individual cells. The intuition: a 2D array (matrix) is essentially an array of arrays. The first index selects which row (inner array), and the second index selects which element within that row. This works because the data is stored in a grid-like structure in memory. The algorithm: given a matrix variable, row index i, and column index j, the element is accessed as matrix[i][j]. First, matrix[i] retrieves the i-th row (a 1D array). Then, [j] retrieves the j-th element from that row. Time and space complexity are O(1) for a single access. This connects to nested iteration by being the operation performed inside the inner loop—once we have indices i and j, we use this to get the element value.
commonMistakes: Using matrix[i,j] instead of matrix[i][j] (invalid syntax in many languages)
Using indices out of bounds (causing errors)
Confusing row-major vs column-major order
explanation.codePattern: element = matrix[i][j]

examples.trace: Step 1: matrix[0] retrieves first row [1,3,5]. Step 2: [1] retrieves second element of that row, which is 3.
Step 1: matrix[1] retrieves second row [40,50,60]. Step 2: [2] retrieves third element of that row, which is 60.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Integer Values
ladders.desc: Check if two integer values are equal using comparison operators.

explanation.prose: A fundamental operation in the search algorithm is comparing two integer values to check for equality. This is the basic operation performed on each element. The intuition is simple: to determine if we've found the target, we need to compare the current element's value with the target value. This works because integers can be directly compared for exact matches. The algorithm: given two integer variables a and b, use the equality operator (== in most languages) to check if a equals b. If true, they match; if false, they don't. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to element access by being the next step—after retrieving an element, we compare it with the target.
commonMistakes: Using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==)
Comparing different data types without conversion
Not considering floating-point precision issues (though here we use integers)
explanation.codePattern: if a == b: # match found

examples.trace: Step 1: Evaluate 5 == 5. Step 2: Since both are 5, the comparison returns True.
Step 1: Evaluate 7 == 3. Step 2: Since 7 and 3 are different, the comparison returns False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a 2D Array Structure
ladders.desc: Recognize that a 2D array is a grid of rows and columns storing values.

explanation.prose: The simplest prerequisite concept for matrix search is understanding what a 2D array (matrix) is. This is the foundation upon which everything else builds. The intuition: a 2D array is a data structure that organizes elements in a grid with rows and columns, similar to a spreadsheet or table. Each element has a unique position identified by two indices: row number and column number. This works because it extends the idea of a 1D array (a list) into two dimensions. For example, a 2×3 matrix has 2 rows and 3 columns, totaling 6 elements. The first index (usually i) selects the row, and the second index (usually j) selects the column. Time and space considerations aren't applicable here as this is conceptual. This connects to element access by defining the structure we're working with—without understanding rows and columns, we can't access elements correctly. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Thinking indices start at 1 instead of 0, 2) Confusing rows with columns, 3) Not realizing each row can have different lengths (though here we assume rectangular matrices).
explanation.codePattern: In Python, a 2D array is a list of lists: matrix = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]] has 2 rows and 3 columns

examples.trace: Step 1: The outer list has 2 elements: [1,2,3] and [4,5,6] → 2 rows. Step 2: First inner list [1,2,3] has 3 elements → 3 columns. Step 3: Element at row 0, column 0 is 1; row 0, column 1 is 2; row 0, column 2 is 3; row 1, column 0 is 4; row 1, column 1 is 5; row 1, column 2 is 6.

examples.output: 3 rows, 2 columns, elements: row0: [10,20], row1: [30,40], row2: [50,60]
examples.trace: Step 1: Outer list has 3 elements → 3 rows. Step 2: Each inner list has 2 elements → 2 columns. Step 3: Positions: (0,0)=10, (0,1)=20, (1,0)=30, (1,1)=40, (2,0)=50, (2,1)=60.

approaches.name: Binary Search per Row O(m*log n)
approaches.summary: For each row in the matrix, perform a binary search to find the target. This method exploits the sorted order within each row but ignores the column-wise sorting.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search Target in Sorted 2D Matrix Using Row-Wise Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution searching for target in sorted matrix by applying binary search to each row.

explanation.prose: This approach leverages the fact that each row in the matrix is sorted in ascending order. While the columns are also sorted, we ignore that property and treat each row as an independent sorted array. For each row, we perform binary search to check if the target exists in that row. The intuition is straightforward: since rows are sorted, binary search gives us O(log n) time per row, and we need to check all m rows, resulting in O(m log n) total time. This method is simple to implement and understand, though not the most optimal for this problem (which could use O(log(mn)) time with a smarter approach). The algorithm iterates through each row from top to bottom. For each row, it checks if the target could possibly be in that row by comparing with the first and last elements (optional optimization to skip rows where target is out of range). Then it performs binary search on that row. If found, return True; if not found after all rows, return False.

connectsTo.reason: This full solution uses the binary search helper function (L1) as its core subroutine

timeComplexity.note: where m is number of rows, n is number of columns

spaceComplexity.note: as we use only constant extra space

commonMistakes: Forgetting to handle empty matrix
Incorrect binary search implementation (off-by-one errors)
Not optimizing by checking row bounds before binary search
Assuming matrix is square
explanation.codePattern: def searchMatrix(matrix, target): if not matrix or not matrix[0]: return False; m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0]); for i in range(m): if matrix[i][0] <= target <= matrix[i][n-1]: left, right = 0, n-1; while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if matrix[i][mid] == target: return True; elif matrix[i][mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return False

examples.trace: Step 1: Check row 0 [1,3,5,7]. Target 3 is between 1 and 7, so proceed. Binary search on row 0: left=0, right=3, mid=1, matrix[0][1]=3 equals target, return True.

Step 1: Row 0 [1,3,5,7]: 13 not between 1 and 7, skip. Step 2: Row 1 [10,11,16,20]: 13 between 10 and 20, binary search: left=0, right=3, mid=1, matrix[1][1]=11 < 13, left=2; mid=2, matrix[1][2]=16 > 13, right=1; left=2 > right=1, stop, not found. Step 3: Row 2 [23,30,34,60]: 13 not between 23 and 60, skip. All rows checked, return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search on a Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to find target in a sorted 1D array.

explanation.prose: Binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding a target value in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition is based on the sorted property: if the middle element equals the target, we're done; if it's less, the target must be in the right half; if it's greater, in the left half. This reduces the problem size by half each step, The algorithm maintains two pointers, left and right, representing the current search interval. Compute mid as the average, compare with target, and adjust pointers accordingly. Continue until left exceeds right (target not found) or target found. Time: O(log n), Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This subroutine is called for each row in the full problem

commonMistakes: Using mid = (left+right)/2 without integer division
Forgetting to update pointers correctly (left = mid+1 or right = mid-
Infinite loops if condition is left < right instead of left <= right
Not handling empty array
explanation.codePattern: def binarySearch(arr, target): left, right = 0, len(arr)-1; while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return True; elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return False

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1, arr[1]=3 equals target, return True.
Step 1: left=0, right=3, mid=1, arr[1]=11 < 13, left=2. Step 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2, arr[2]=16 > 13, right=1. Step 3: left=2 > right=1, stop, return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Over Rows and Applying Search per Row
ladders.desc: Process each row of a 2D matrix and apply a search function to each.

explanation.prose: This level focuses on the high-level logic of handling a 2D matrix by treating each row as an independent entity. The intuition is that a 2D matrix can be decomposed into multiple 1D arrays (rows), and we can apply a search operation to each row sequentially. This is a common pattern in matrix problems: iterate through rows using a loop, and for each row, perform some operation. In our approach, the operation is binary search, but this logic is generic. The algorithm: 1) Get dimensions m (rows) and n (columns), 2) Loop i from 0 to m-1, 3) For each row i, check if target could be in that row (optional optimization by comparing with first and last elements), 4) Apply search function to row i, 5) If found, return early; if not, continue. Time: O(m * time_per_row), Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This uses binary search (L1) as the search function

commonMistakes: Not handling empty matrix
Incorrect row indexing
Forgetting to return early when found
Not optimizing with row bounds check
explanation.codePattern: def searchRows(matrix, target): m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0]); for i in range(m): if matrix[i][0] <= target <= matrix[i][n-1]: if binarySearch(matrix[i], target): return True; return False

examples.trace: Step 1: Row 0: check bounds 1<=3<=7 true, apply binary search on [1,3,5,7], finds 3, return True.
Step 1: Row 0: 1<=13<=7 false, skip. Step 2: Row 1: 10<=13<=20 true, binary search on [10,11,16,20], not found. Step 3: Row 2: 23<=13<=60 false, skip. All rows done, return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Accessing and Comparing Elements in a 2D Matrix
ladders.desc: Learn to navigate a 2D matrix, access elements, and compare values.

explanation.prose: This level covers fundamental operations on a 2D matrix (list of lists in Python). The intuition is that a matrix is a grid where each element is accessed using two indices: row index and column index. For example, matrix[i][j] gives the element at row i, column j. Key concepts: 1) Dimensions: number of rows m = len(matrix), number of columns n = len(matrix[0]) if matrix non-empty, 2) Iteration: use nested loops or single loop over rows, 3) Comparison: compare elements with target to make decisions. In our approach, we need to access the first and last elements of each row to optimize search. The algorithm: 1) Check if matrix is empty, 2) Get m and n, 3) For each row i, access matrix[i][0] (first) and matrix[i][n-1] (last), 4) Compare with target to decide if target could be in that row. Time: O(m) for accessing, Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This is used in the row bounds check in L2

commonMistakes: Index out of bounds when accessing matrix[0] if matrix empty
Assuming all rows have same length
Incorrect column index for last element
Not using zero-based indexing
explanation.codePattern: def checkRowBounds(matrix, target): if not matrix: return False; m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0]); for i in range(m): first = matrix[i][0]; last = matrix[i][n-1]; if first <= target <= last: return i; return -1

examples.trace: Step 1: m=3, n=4. Row 0: first=1, last=7, 1<=3<=7 true, return row index 0.

Step 1: m=3, n=4. Row 0: 1<=13<=7 false. Row 1: 10<=13<=20 true, return row index 1. (Note: This example returns 1, but for illustration, if we check all rows without early return, after row 1, we might continue, but function returns first match; to show -1, assume target=100: Row 0: 1<=100<=7 false, Row 1: 10<=100<=20 false, Row 2: 23<=100<=60 false, return -1.)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Looping Through Indices and Conditional Checks
ladders.desc: Master loops and if-conditions to traverse data structures and make decisions.

explanation.prose: This level focuses on basic programming constructs: loops and conditional statements. The intuition is that to process collections like arrays or matrices, we use loops to iterate through elements, and conditionals to make decisions based on values. A for loop iterates a fixed number of times (e.g., over indices), while a while loop continues until a condition is false. Conditionals (if, elif, else) allow branching. In our approach, we use a for loop to go through each row index, and if statements to check bounds. The algorithm: 1) Initialize index i = 0, 2) While i < number of rows, 3) Access row i, 4) If condition met (e.g., target in range), perform action, 5) Increment i. Time: O(m) for loop, Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This is used to implement the row iteration in L3

commonMistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop range
Forgetting to update loop variable
Incorrect conditional logic
Using break or return incorrectly
explanation.codePattern: def loopExample(matrix, target): m = len(matrix); for i in range(m): row = matrix[i]; if row[0] <= target <= row[-1]: print('Target possibly in row', i); else: print('Skip row', i)

examples.output: Target possibly in row 0, Skip row 1, Skip row 2
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, row=[1,3,5,7], 1<=3<=7 true, print 'Target possibly in row 0'. Step 2: i=1, row=[10,11,16,20], 10<=3<=20 false, print 'Skip row 1'. Step 3: i=2, row=[23,30,34,60], 23<=3<=60 false, print 'Skip row 2'.

examples.output: Skip row 0, Target possibly in row 1, Skip row 2

Skip row 0, Target possibly in row 1, Skip row 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Basic Comparisons
ladders.desc: Learn what arrays are and how to compare numbers.

explanation.prose: This level introduces the most basic concepts: arrays and comparisons. An array is an ordered collection of elements, like a list of numbers. In Python, it's represented as a list, e.g., [1, 3, 5, 7]. Each element has an index starting from 0. Comparisons involve operators like == (equal), != (not equal), < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal). The intuition is that we can store data in arrays and use comparisons to make decisions. For example, to check if a target number is in an array, we might compare each element. In our approach, we use comparisons to check if target is within the range of a row. The algorithm: 1) Define an array, 2) Define a target number, 3) Compare target with array elements using a loop or directly. Time: O(1) for simple comparisons, Space: O(1).

connectsTo.reason: This is the foundation for the conditional checks in L4

commonMistakes: Confusing array indices
Using wrong comparison operators
Not understanding that arrays can be empty
explanation.codePattern: arr = [1, 3, 5, 7]; target = 3; if target >= arr[0] and target <= arr[-1]: print('Target in range'); else: print('Target out of range')

examples.trace: Step 1: arr[0] = 1, arr[-1] = 7. Step 2: Check 3 >= 1 true, 3 <= 7 true, so print 'Target in range'.
Step 1: arr[0] = 10, arr[-1] = 20. Step 2: Check 13 >= 10 true, 13 <= 20 true, so print 'Target in range'.

approaches.name: Search from Top-Right Corner O(m+n)
approaches.summary: Start at the top-right corner of the matrix and move left or down based on comparisons with the target. This leverages both row and column sorting to eliminate rows or columns in each step.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Sorted 2D Matrix using Top-Right Corner Approach
ladders.desc: Complete implementation searching for a target in a sorted matrix by starting at top-right corner and moving left/down.

explanation.prose: This approach leverages the matrix's sorted properties: each row is sorted left-to-right, and each column is sorted top-to-bottom. Starting at the top-right corner (row 0, column n-1) gives us a strategic position. At each step, we compare the current element with the target. If they're equal, we've found the target. If the current element is greater than the target, we can eliminate the entire current column because all elements below in that column are even larger (due to column sorting). So we move left (column--). If the current element is smaller than the target, we can eliminate the entire current row because all elements to the left in that row are even smaller (due to row sorting). So we move down (row++). We continue until we either find the target or go out of bounds, indicating the target isn't present. Intuition: Think of the matrix as having a 'staircase' property. Starting at top-right gives us maximum elimination power - each comparison eliminates either an entire row or an entire column. This is more efficient than binary search on each row (O(m log n)) or treating the matrix as a 1D array (O(log(mn)) but with more complex indexing).

connectsTo.reason: This full solution uses the core movement logic from L1 as its main loop

timeComplexity.note: In worst case, we traverse from top-right to bottom-left corner

commonMistakes: Starting at wrong corner (top-left or bottom-right) - these don't give the elimination property
Forgetting to check bounds - can lead to index errors
Using wrong comparison directions - remember: > target means move left, < target means move down
algorithm: a. If matrix[row][col] == target: return True
b. If matrix[row][col] > target: col-- (move left)

examples.trace: Start at (0,3) = 11 > 9 → move left to (0,2) = 7 < 9 → move down to (1,2) = 8 < 9 → move down to (2,2) = 9 == 9 → Found! Return True
Start at (0,3) = 11 < 15 → move down to (1,3) = 12 < 15 → move down to (2,3) = 16 > 15 → move left to (2,2) = 9 < 15 → move down to (3,2) = 14 < 15 → move down to row=4 (out of bounds) → Return False

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Matrix Traversal with Elimination Logic
ladders.desc: Implement the core movement logic that eliminates rows/columns based on comparisons.

explanation.prose:
This sub-routine implements the heart of the algorithm: given a starting position at the top-right corner, it systematically moves through the matrix by comparing the current element with the target and deciding whether to move left or down. The key insight is that each comparison allows us to eliminate either an entire row or an entire column from further consideration. Why this works: The matrix has two sorting properties - rows are sorted left-to-right, and columns are sorted top-to-bottom. When we're at position (row, col):
1. All elements to the left in the same row are ≤ current element (row sorting)
2. All elements below in the same column are ≥ current element (column sorting) So if current element > target, we know the target cannot be in the current column (everything below is even larger). If current element < target, we know the target cannot be in the current row (everything left is even smaller).

connectsTo.reason: This is the main loop of the full solution

timeComplexity.note:
for an m×n matrix
Space Complexity: O(1)


Connection to L2: This implements the elimination strategy from L2

commonMistakes: Moving in wrong direction based on comparison
Not handling empty matrix edge case
Using ≤ or ≥ instead of strict comparisons
algorithm: a. Compare current element with target
3. Return failure if out of bounds

examples.trace: (0,2)=3 < 5 → move down to (1,2)=6 > 5 → move left to (1,1)=5 == 5 → Found!
(0,2)=30 > 25 → move left to (0,1)=20 < 25 → move down to (1,1)=50 > 25 → move left to (1,0)=40 > 25 → move left to col=-1 (out of bounds) → Not found

ladders.title: Core Logic — Elimination Strategy in Sorted Matrix
ladders.desc: Understand how to eliminate rows/columns using matrix sorting properties.

explanation.prose:
This level focuses on the core insight: in a matrix with rows sorted left-to-right and columns sorted top-to-bottom, we can systematically eliminate either entire rows or entire columns based on a single comparison. This is the fundamental reasoning that makes the O(m+n) algorithm possible. Intuition: Imagine you're looking for a specific number in a sorted list. If you check the middle element and it's too large, you know your target must be in the left half. If it's too small, it must be in the right half. The 2D version extends this idea in two dimensions. When we check an element at position (i,j):
1. All elements in the same row to the left are ≤ matrix[i][j] (because row is sorted left-to-right)
2. All elements in the same column below are ≥ matrix[i][j] (because column is sorted top-to-bottom) Therefore:
- If matrix[i][j] > target: Target cannot be in column j (everything below is even larger)
- If matrix[i][j] < target: Target cannot be in row i (everything left is even smaller)
- If matrix[i][j] == target: We found it! The strategic choice of starting position matters. Top-right corner (0, n-1) is optimal because:
1. It's the largest element in row 0
2. It's the smallest element in column n-1
This gives us maximum elimination power from the start. Algorithm thinking:
1. Start at top-right
2. At each step, compare current element with target
3. Eliminate current column if current > target (move left)
4. Eliminate current row if current < target (move down)
5. Repeat until found or eliminated all possibilities Time Analysis: Each comparison eliminates one row OR one column, so maximum m+n steps. Connection to L3: This builds upon understanding matrix sorting properties from L3. Real code pattern (conceptual):
# The elimination logic:
if current > target: # Eliminate current column - all elements below are even larger move_left()
elif current < target: # Eliminate current row - all elements left are even smaller move_down()
else: # Found target return True.

connectsTo.reason: This elimination logic is what drives the traversal in L1

commonMistakes: Not recognizing why top-right is the optimal starting point
Confusing row vs column elimination conditions
Trying to eliminate both row and column simultaneously (can't do both)

examples.trace: Current element 8 > target 6 → Can we eliminate column? Check: All elements below in column 2 are [8,9], both ≥ 8 > 6 → Yes, eliminate column 2 → Move left to column 1
Current element 4 < target 7 → Can we eliminate row? Check: All elements left in row 0 are [1], both ≤ 4 < 7 → Yes, eliminate row 0 → Move down to row 1

Position (2,1)=6 in matrix [[1,4,7,11],[2,5,8,12],[3,6,9,16]], target=9, what to eliminate?
Position (1,2)=8 in matrix [[1,4,7],[2,5,8],[3,6,9]], target=8, what to do?
Position (0,0)=1 in matrix [[1,2],[3,4]], target=4, what to eliminate?
Position (1,1)=5 in matrix [[1,3,5],[2,4,6]], target=3, what to eliminate?
Position (0,2)=15 in matrix [[5,10,15],[20,25,30]], target=12, what to eliminate?
Position (1,0)=20 in matrix [[5,10,15],[20,25,30]], target=25, what to eliminate?
Position (0,1)=100 in matrix [[50,100],[150,200]], target=150, what to eliminate?
Position (1,1)=0 in matrix [[-5,-3,0],[2,4,6]], target=-3, what to eliminate?

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Sorted Matrix Properties
ladders.desc: Learn how row and column sorting constraints create search opportunities.

explanation.prose:
This level establishes the foundational understanding of what makes the search algorithm possible: the specific sorting properties of the matrix. A 'sorted matrix' in this context means:
1. Each row is sorted in non-decreasing order from left to right
2. Each column is sorted in non-decreasing order from top to bottom These two constraints together create a powerful structure. They don't mean the entire matrix is fully sorted (it's not), but they create predictable relationships between elements. Key implications:
1. For any element matrix[i][j]: - All elements in the same row to the left are ≤ matrix[i][j] - All elements in the same row to the right are ≥ matrix[i][j] - All elements in the same column above are ≤ matrix[i][j] - All elements in the same column below are ≥ matrix[i][j] 2. The matrix has a 'staircase' property: as you move right in a row, values increase; as you move down in a column, values increase. 3. The smallest element is at top-left (0,0) and the largest is at bottom-right (m-1,n-1). Why this matters for search: These properties allow us to make elimination decisions. If we're looking for a target and we check an element:
- If the element is larger than target, we know the target cannot be in the same column below (those are even larger)
- If the element is smaller than target, we know the target cannot be in the same row to the left (those are even smaller) Visualization: Draw a 3×3 matrix and trace the sorted properties. Notice that while the entire matrix isn't sorted, you can think of it as having sorted 'stripes'. Connection to L4: This builds on basic comparison operations from L4. Real code pattern (verification):
def isMatrixSorted(matrix): m, n = len(matrix), len(matrix[0]) # Check rows for i in range(m): for j in range(1, n): if matrix[i][j] < matrix[i][j-1]: return False # Check columns for j in range(n): for i in range(1, m): if matrix[i][j] < matrix[i-1][j]: return False return True.

connectsTo.reason: These properties enable the elimination strategy in L2

commonMistakes: Assuming the entire matrix is sorted (it's not - try reading it row by row)
Not recognizing that both constraints must hold
Confusing the direction of sorting (rows left-to-right, columns top-to-bottom)

examples.input: Matrix [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]] - what can we say about element at (1,1)=5?
examples.output: Elements left in row: 4≤5, Elements right in row: 6≥5, Elements above: 2≤5, Elements below: 8≥5
examples.trace: Row 1: left=4, right=6 → 4≤5≤6 ✓, Column 1: above=2, below=8 → 2≤5≤8 ✓

testCases.input: Matrix [[1,3,5],[2,4,6],[7,8,9]], is it sorted by problem definition?
Matrix [[1,2,3],[3,4,5],[5,6,7]], is it sorted by problem definition?
Matrix [[1,5,3],[2,6,4],[3,7,5]], is it sorted by problem definition?
In matrix [[10,20,30],[40,50,60]], for element at (0,1)=20, what are all elements ≤ it?
In matrix [[10,20,30],[40,50,60]], for element at (1,1)=50, what are all elements ≥ it?

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Matrix Navigation and Element Comparison
ladders.desc: Master accessing matrix elements and making comparison decisions.

explanation.prose:
This level focuses on the fundamental operations needed for the search algorithm: accessing specific elements in a 2D matrix and comparing them with a target value. These are the building blocks that will be combined in higher levels. Matrix Access Fundamentals:
A 2D matrix is typically represented as a list of lists in programming. If we have matrix with m rows and n columns:
- matrix[i][j] accesses the element at row i, column j
- Row indices go from 0 to m-1
- Column indices go from 0 to n-1
- The top-left corner is matrix[0][0]
- The top-right corner is matrix[0][n-1]
- The bottom-left corner is matrix[m-1][0]
- The bottom-right corner is matrix[m-1][n-1] Navigation Operations:
1. Move right: column index increases (j++)
2. Move left: column index decreases (j--)
3. Move down: row index increases (i++)
4. Move up: row index decreases (i--) Comparison Logic:
Given a current value and a target:
1. If current == target: Found what we're looking for
2. If current > target: Current value is too large
3. If current < target: Current value is too small In the context of our algorithm:
- We'll start at top-right: row=0, col=n-1
- If current > target: Move left (col--)
- If current < target: Move down (row++) Boundary Checking:
We must always check that our indices are valid:
- row must satisfy: 0 ≤ row < m
- col must satisfy: 0 ≤ col < n
If either condition fails, we've gone out of bounds. Connection to L5: This builds on basic programming concepts from L5.

connectsTo.reason: These operations are applied to matrices with the sorting properties from L3

commonMistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices
Forgetting to check bounds before accessing
Confusing row and column indices
Using wrong comparison operators
explanation.codePattern:
# Initialize at top-right
row = 0
col = len(matrix[0]) - 1  # n-1

# Access current element
current = matrix[row][col]

# Compare and decide
if current == target:
    return True
elif current > target:
    col -= 1  # Move left
else:
    row += 1  # Move down

# Check bounds
if row >= len(matrix) or col < 0:
    return False  # Out of bounds

examples.input: Matrix [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]], start at (0,2), compare with target=5
examples.trace: Access matrix[0][2] = 3, Compare: 3 < 5 → move down: row becomes 1, col stays 2 → new position (1,2)

examples.input: Matrix [[10,20,30],[40,50,60]], start at (0,2), compare with target=25
examples.trace: Access matrix[0][2] = 30, Compare: 30 > 25 → move left: row stays 0, col becomes 1 → new position (0,1)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays, Indexing, and Comparisons
ladders.desc: Learn basic array operations and comparison logic.

explanation.prose:
This is the foundational level that establishes the basic programming concepts needed for the matrix search algorithm. We'll cover three core concepts: 1) Arrays/Lists, 2) Indexing, and 3) Comparison Operations. 1. Arrays/Lists:
An array (called a list in Python) is an ordered collection of elements. Example: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Arrays have:
- A length (number of elements)
- Elements accessible by position (index)
- The first element is at index 0, last at index length-1 2. Indexing:
Indexing means accessing a specific element by its position. If arr = [10, 20, 30, 40]:
- arr[0] = 10 (first element)
- arr[1] = 20 (second element)
- arr[3] = 40 (last element)
- arr[-1] = 40 (also last element, Python specific) For 2D arrays (matrices), we use two indices: matrix[row][column]. 3. Comparison Operations:
We compare values using these operators:
- == (equal to): 5 == 5 is True, 5 == 3 is False
- != (not equal to): 5 != 3 is True, 5 != 5 is False
- > (greater than): 5 > 3 is True, 3 > 5 is False
- < (less than): 3 < 5 is True, 5 < 3 is False
- >= (greater than or equal): 5 >= 5 is True, 5 >= 3 is True
- <= (less than or equal): 3 <= 5 is True, 5 <= 5 is True In our algorithm, we mainly use ==, >, and <. Putting it together:
Let's say we have matrix = [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]] and we want to access the element in the first row, third column:
- matrix[0][2] = 3 If we compare this with target = 5:
- 3 == 5? False
- 3 > 5? False
- 3 < 5? True So we would determine that 3 is less than 5. Common beginner mistakes:
1. Thinking array indices start at 1 (they start at 0)
2. Confusing = (assignment) with == (comparison)
3. Not understanding that > and < are strict comparisons
4. Trying to access indices outside the array bounds Real code examples:
# 1D array example
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40]
print(arr[0]) # Output: 10
print(arr[2]) # Output: 30 # Comparison examples
a = 5
b = 3
print(a == b) # False
print(a > b) # True
print(a < b) # False # 2D array (matrix)
matrix = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
print(matrix[0][1]) # Output: 2 (row 0, column 1)
print(matrix[1][0]) # Output: 3 (row 1, column 0).

connectsTo.reason: These basic operations are used to navigate and compare in the matrix in L4

examples.input: Array [5, 10, 15, 20], access element at index 2, compare with value 12
Matrix [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]], access element at row 1, column 1, compare with 5
examples.trace: matrix[1][1] = 5, Compare: 5 == 5? Yes → Found match!

testCases.input: Array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], what is element at index 0?
Array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], what is element at index 4?
Matrix [[10,20],[30,40]], what is element at row 0, column 1?
Matrix [[10,20],[30,40]], what is element at row 1, column 0?

approaches.name: Treat Matrix as Sorted Array O(log(m*n))
approaches.summary: Flatten the matrix conceptually into a single sorted array and perform binary search. Map the 1D index to 2D coordinates using division and modulus operations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Search in Sorted 2D Matrix Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution to search for a target value in a sorted 2D matrix by treating it as a single sorted array.

explanation.prose: The intuition behind this approach is that a matrix sorted row-wise with each row's first element greater than the last element of the previous row behaves like a single sorted array when flattened. This property allows us to apply binary search directly on the conceptual flattened array. We maintain two pointers, left and right, representing the start and end indices of this virtual array. At each step, we calculate the middle index, map it to 2D coordinates using division and modulus operations with the number of columns, and compare the element at that position with the target. If the element equals the target, we return true. If it's less, we search the right half; if greater, we search the left half. The algorithm terminates when left exceeds right, indicating the target is not present. The .

connectsTo.reason: by relying on the index-to-coordinate mapping subroutine

timeComplexity.note: ) because we perform binary search on m*n elements, and space complexity is O(1) as we use only a few variables

commonMistakes: incorrect mapping formulas (e.g
using rows instead of columns for modulus) or off-by-one errors in binary search bounds
explanation.codePattern: Initialize left=0, right=m*n-1; while left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2; row=mid//n; col=mid%n; if matrix[row][col]==target: return True; elif matrix[row][col]<target: left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1; return False

examples.trace: Step 1: m=3, n=4, left=0, right=11. Step 2: mid=(0+11)//2=5, row=5//4=1, col=5%4=1, matrix[1][1]=11 < 13, so left=mid+1=6. Step 3: mid=(6+11)//2=8, row=8//4=2, col=8%4=0, matrix[2][0]=23 > 13, so right=mid-1=7. Step 4: mid=(6+7)//2=6, row=6//4=1, col=6%4=2, matrix[1][2]=16 > 13, so right=mid-1=5. Step 5: left=6 > right=5, loop ends, return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Map 1D Index to 2D Coordinates
ladders.desc: Convert a 1D index in a flattened matrix to its corresponding row and column in the 2D matrix.

explanation.prose: This subroutine is crucial because binary search operates on a conceptual 1D array, but we need to access elements in the actual 2D matrix. The intuition is that if we imagine flattening the matrix row by row, each 1D index corresponds to a specific position: the row is determined by how many full rows fit into the index (using integer division by the number of columns), and the column is the remainder (using modulus). For a matrix with n columns, row = index // n and col = index % n. This works because each row has n elements, so dividing by n gives the row number, and the remainder gives the column offset. The time and space complexity are O(1) as it involves constant-time arithmetic. This feeds into L0 by enabling element access during binary search.
commonMistakes: swapping rows
columns in the formulas or using the wrong divisor (e.g
number of rows instead of columns)
explanation.codePattern: Given index and n (columns), row = index // n, col = index % n

examples.input: matrix with 3 rows and 4 columns, index = 5
examples.trace: Step 1: n=4. Step 2: row = 5 // 4 = 1 (since 4 fits into 5 once). Step 3: col = 5 % 4 = 1 (remainder of 5 divided by 4). Step 4: In matrix, index 5 corresponds to element at row 1, column 1.

examples.input: matrix with 2 rows and 3 columns, index = 4
examples.trace: Step 1: n=3. Step 2: row = 4 // 3 = 1 (since 3 fits into 4 once). Step 3: col = 4 % 3 = 1 (remainder of 4 divided by 3). Step 4: In matrix, index 4 corresponds to element at row 1, column 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Conceptual Flattened Array
ladders.desc: Perform binary search on a sorted array that is conceptually derived from the matrix.

explanation.prose: The core insight is that the sorted matrix can be treated as a single sorted array due to its row-wise and column-wise ordering. We define left and right pointers for the start and end indices of this virtual array (0 to m*n-1). At each step, we calculate the middle index, but instead of accessing an actual 1D array, we use the mapping from L1 to get the corresponding matrix element. We then compare this element with the target: if equal, we found it; if less, the target must be in the right half (update left); if greater, in the left half (update right). This reduces the search space by half each time, leading to logarithmic time complexity. The intuition is that binary search relies on sorted order, and the matrix's properties ensure the flattened version is sorted.

connectsTo.reason: by requiring the index mapping to access elements

timeComplexity.note: ) for the search, and space is O(1)

commonMistakes: incorrect termination conditions (e.g
using left < right instead of left <= right) or not updating bounds correctly
explanation.codePattern: left=0, right=total_elements-1; while left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2; element=get_element(mid); if element==target: return True; elif element<target: left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1; return False

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=11. Step 2: mid=(0+11)//2=5, element=11 > 3, so right=mid-1=4. Step 3: mid=(0+4)//2=2, element=5 > 3, so right=mid-1=1. Step 4: mid=(0+1)//2=0, element=1 < 3, so left=mid+1=1. Step 5: mid=(1+1)//2=1, element=3 == 3, return true.

Step 1: left=0, right=11. Step 2: mid=(0+11)//2=5, element=11 < 13, so left=mid+1=6. Step 3: mid=(6+11)//2=8, element=23 > 13, so right=mid-1=7. Step 4: mid=(6+7)//2=6, element=16 > 13, so right=mid-1=5. Step 5: left=6 > right=5, loop ends, return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Matrix Flattening Concept
ladders.desc: Understand how a 2D matrix can be represented as a 1D array by concatenating rows.

explanation.prose: This intermediate concept involves mentally converting a 2D matrix into a 1D array by placing all elements in row-major order: first all elements of the first row, then the second row, and so on. For a matrix with m rows and n columns, the flattened array has m*n elements. The intuition is that this transformation preserves the sorted order if the matrix is sorted row-wise with each row's first element greater than the last element of the previous row. This is key because it allows us to apply 1D algorithms like binary search. The connection to L2 is that binary search operates on this flattened representation. Time and space analysis: conceptual only, no actual flattening is done, so O(1) space.
commonMistakes: assuming column-major order or not verifying the sorted property
explanation.codePattern: Conceptually, for matrix[i][j] in row i and column j, its 1D index = i*n + j

examples.trace: Step 1: m=2, n=2. Step 2: Row 0: elements 1,2. Step 3: Row 1: elements 3,4. Step 4: Concatenate: [1,2,3,4]. Step 5: Index mapping: matrix[0][0]=1 at index 0, matrix[0][1]=2 at index 1, matrix[1][0]=3 at index 2, matrix[1][1]=4 at index 3.

Step 1: m=2, n=3. Step 2: Row 0: elements 5,6,7. Step 3: Row 1: elements 8,9,10. Step 4: Concatenate: [5,6,7,8,9,10]. Step 5: Index mapping: matrix[0][0]=5 at index 0, matrix[0][1]=6 at index 1, matrix[0][2]=7 at index 2, matrix[1][0]=8 at index 3, matrix[1][1]=9 at index 4, matrix[1][2]=10 at index 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulus
ladders.desc: Perform division and modulus operations to extract quotient and remainder.

explanation.prose: This fundamental operation involves dividing two integers to get a quotient (using //) and a remainder (using %). The intuition is that for non-negative integers a and b, a // b gives how many times b fits into a (floor division), and a % b gives what's left over. In the context of matrix mapping, if we have an index in a flattened array and n columns, index // n tells us which row the element is in (since each row has n elements), and index % n tells us the column position. This is essential for converting between 1D and 2D representations. Time and space complexity are O(1).
commonMistakes: confusing // with / (which gives float) or misunderstanding negative numbers (but indices are non-negative here)
explanation.codePattern: quotient = a // b, remainder = a % b

examples.trace: Step 1: 4 fits into 5 once, so quotient = 5 // 4 = 1. Step 2: Remainder = 5 - 1*4 = 1, so 5 % 4 = 1. Step 3: This means 5 divided by 4 gives 1 with remainder 1.
Step 1: 3 fits into 8 twice (since 2*3=6), so quotient = 8 // 3 = 2. Step 2: Remainder = 8 - 2*3 = 2, so 8 % 3 = 2. Step 3: This means 8 divided by 3 gives 2 with remainder 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices, starting from 0.

explanation.prose: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding that arrays are ordered collections where each element has a position called an index, typically starting at 0. For example, in an array arr = [10, 20, 30], arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, and arr[2] = 30. The intuition is that indices allow direct access to elements without scanning the entire array. In the context of matrices, we extend this to 2D indexing: matrix[row][col] accesses the element at the given row and column. This foundation is essential for all higher levels because we need to retrieve matrix elements during search. Time complexity for access is O(1), and .

connectsTo.reason: by being the basic operation we perform after computing coordinates

commonMistakes: using 1-based indexing or accessing out-of-bounds indices
explanation.codePattern: element = array[index] for 1D, or element = matrix[row][col] for 2D

examples.trace: Step 1: Array indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15, 3->20. Step 2: Access index 2: array[2] = 15. Step 3: This is the third element in the array.
Step 1: Matrix rows: row 0 has [1,2], row 1 has [3,4]. Step 2: Access row 1, column 0: matrix[1][0] = 3. Step 3: This is the element in the second row, first column.
